_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: File I/O practice 1
questions.prompt: Which type represents a file-system path?
questions.explanation: `Path` represents a location and can be passed to Files operations. A String contains characters but does not provide the file-system abstraction.

questions.title: File I/O practice 2
choices: It prevents IOException at compile time
It converts the file to an array
questions.explanation: The stream holds an open file resource. Try-with-resources calls its close method even when processing throws an exception.

questions.title: Trace File I/O
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The same Path is used for writing and reading. UTF-8 makes the text encoding an explicit part of the file format. The resulting output is 'CS101\nMATH120'.

questions.title: File I/O practice 5
questions.prompt: What do the CREATE and APPEND options change about `Files.writeString`?
choices: New text is added to the end of the file instead of replacing what is there
The file is deleted first, then rewritten
The write is retried automatically if it fails
The text is compressed before writing
questions.explanation: By default `writeString` truncates: it replaces the whole file. `APPEND` makes it add to the end, and `CREATE` makes the file first if it does not exist. Without APPEND the earlier contents are lost. The options do not delete, retry, or compress.

questions.title: File I/O concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Default writing replaces contents"?
choices: A successful write can still be the wrong action. If old records matter, include the append option or read, update, and rewrite the complete data intentionally.
questions.explanation: Use `Path` with `Files.readString` and `Files.writeString` for straightforward text files. File methods can throw IOException, so catch it where recovery is possible or declare it with `throws`. That reasoning supports the answer: A successful write can still be the wrong action. If old records matter, include the append option or read, update, and rewrite the complete data intentionally.

questions.title: File I/O concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable File I/O practice?
choices: It prevents IOException at compile time
It converts the file to an array
questions.explanation: Use `Path` with `Files.readString` and `Files.writeString` for straightforward text files. File methods can throw IOException, so catch it where recovery is possible or declare it with `throws`. That reasoning supports the answer: Represent locations with Path.

questions.title: File I/O concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of File I/O code?
questions.explanation: Use `Path` with `Files.readString` and `Files.writeString` for straightforward text files. File methods can throw IOException, so catch it where recovery is possible or declare it with `throws`. That reasoning supports the answer: Select UTF-8 for portable text.

questions.title: File I/O concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common File I/O mistake?
choices: Decide whether a write replaces or appends.
It prevents IOException at compile time
It converts the file to an array
questions.explanation: Use `Path` with `Files.readString` and `Files.writeString` for straightforward text files. File methods can throw IOException, so catch it where recovery is possible or declare it with `throws`. That reasoning supports the answer: Decide whether a write replaces or appends.

questions.title: File I/O concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of File I/O. Which advice is correct?
choices: Handle IOException at a layer that can respond.
It prevents IOException at compile time
questions.explanation: Use `Path` with `Files.readString` and `Files.writeString` for straightforward text files. File methods can throw IOException, so catch it where recovery is possible or declare it with `throws`. That reasoning supports the answer: Handle IOException at a layer that can respond.

questions.title: File I/O concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about File I/O?
choices: It converts the file to an array
Stream large files and close the stream automatically.
questions.explanation: Use `Path` with `Files.readString` and `Files.writeString` for straightforward text files. File methods can throw IOException, so catch it where recovery is possible or declare it with `throws`. That reasoning supports the answer: Stream large files and close the stream automatically.
